params.text:
Further categories to know

Alongside the categories on the previous page, the guidance also highlights forms of abuse that can be hidden or harder to recognise. Frontline care workers are well placed to notice small changes that point to these.

  • Domestic abuse - abuse between people in a relationship or family, including controlling and coercive behaviour, not only physical violence.
  • Sexual abuse - any sexual activity a person has not consented to, could not consent to, or was pressured into.
  • Modern slavery - human trafficking, forced labour and domestic servitude, where a person is exploited and controlled by others.
  • Discriminatory abuse - harassment, slurs or unequal treatment based on a protected characteristic such as age, disability, race, religion, sex or sexual orientation.

Recognising the signs

Indicators vary between categories, but common warning signs include unexplained injuries or frequent falls, sudden changes in mood or withdrawal, fearfulness around certain people, unexplained money problems, poor hygiene or weight loss, and reluctance to be left alone with someone. A single sign does not prove abuse, but a pattern of signs, or a sign that does not fit the explanation given, should always prompt you to raise a concern.

params.question: You help support Mrs Okoro at home. Over a few weeks you notice she has become withdrawn, flinches when her nephew visits, and money seems to be disappearing from her purse. What is the best action?

answers.text: Record exactly what you have seen and report your safeguarding concern to your manager or safeguarding lead straight away.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Several indicators together suggest possible abuse. You record the facts and report promptly so the right people can act.

answers.text: Ask the nephew directly whether he has been taking her money.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Confronting a possible perpetrator can put Mrs Okoro at greater risk and could interfere with any later enquiry.

answers.text: Wait a few more weeks to be certain before saying anything.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Delay can leave the person at continued risk. You do not need to be certain - a reasonable concern is enough to report.

answers.text: Decide it is a family matter and none of your business.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Where an adult with care needs may be at risk, it is your responsibility to raise it, not to look away.
